Это открытие приближает нас к пониманию интенсивных электрических разрядов, наблюдаемых во время извержений вулканов, таких как извержение Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha‘apai в 2022 году.

bf73dc1d763a.title: Mud-rich coastline made 2011 Japan tsunami far more destructive, study finds

Scientists think they are looking at chemical building blocks of life preserved for 3.5bn years

Nasa’s Curiosity rover has detected organic molecules on Mars, including chemicals widely considered building blocks for the origin of life on Earth.

6cddbd42dd77.title: Zoologist, author and presenter Desmond Morris dies aged 98
6cddbd42dd77.summary: Известный зоолог, автор и телеведущий Дезмонд Моррис скончался в возрасте 98 лет. Он прославился благодаря книге "Обнажённый человек" и программе "Zoo Time" на ITV, а также своей работой на стыке зоологии и сюрреализма. Его сын отметил не только профессиональные достижения, но и его роль как отца и дедушки.
6cddbd42dd77.original_description:
Morris pursued dual passions of zoology and surrealist art, presenting TV documentaries and hosting exhibitions

The zoologist Desmond Morris, perhaps best known for his book The Naked Ape and his work on the ITV programme Zoo Time, has died aged 98.

Morris’s son Jason paid tribute to him after his death on Sunday, praising his many professional achievements as well as his role as a father and grandfather.

7b4465aa5ec1.title: Cocaine pollution in rivers and lakes may disrupt behaviour of salmon, study finds
7b4465aa5ec1.summary: Исследование показало, что попадание кокаина в водные объекты может влиять на поведение лососей. Эксперименты с молодым атлантическим лососем показали, что воздействие наркотика и его метаболита приводит к увеличению дистанции перемещения и распространения рыбы, что может иметь непредсказуемые последствия для популяций лосося.
7b4465aa5ec1.original_description:

Traces of cocaine that pollute rivers and lakes may accumulate in the brains of salmon and disrupt their behaviour, according to researchers who warn of unknown consequences for fish populations.

First recorded in 687BC, the meteoroids were once part of the tail of a comet discovered in 1861

16cf430d2896.summary:
All good? Busy day? Small talk is a social good with a bad reputation. We dread it, but it’s vital for human connection

Hi there, how’re you? How’s it going? You alright? All good?

As any Briton knows, none of these questions is an inquiry into your emotional state, the material conditions of your life or your opinion on anything. Respond positively – “all good so far, touch wood” is nice – then move on to the purpose of the interaction: “I’m returning an Amazon package?”

We send the voice of the dead across space as an act of continuity and care, while on Earth we tally the bodies. Which do we choose to become?

В то же время, некоторые эксперты выражают сомнения в заявленной степени возможностей модели, что вызывает вопросы о мотивах компании – является ли это реальной обеспокоенностью безопасностью или же PR-ходом.

d9042ec2c01c.title: The surprising value of boring chats, ‘super El Niño’ and Alzheimer’s evidence reviewed – podcast

560a4b28f71a.title: Helium: the invisible gas that powers AI, and why it’s in short supply – podcast
560a4b28f71a.summary: Alongside the oil and gas stranded in the strait of Hormuz is another commodity vital to today’s economy: helium. It is a critical element in all kinds of areas from MRI machines to the Large Hadron Collider, and even deep-sea diving. It is also integral to the AI boom. And this isn’t the first time its fragile global supply chain has been threatened. So why is helium so useful, and what will happen if the shortage continues? Ian Sample hears from co-host Madeleine Finlay, and from Sophia Hay

Zoologist, author and broadcaster who found fame with his groundbreaking 1967 book The Naked Ape

d23a3ac3d694.title: ‘Oscar of science’ awarded to team behind gene therapy that restores lost vision
d23a3ac3d694.summary: Ученые Жан Беннетт и Альберт Магуайр, создавшие генную терапию Luxturna, восстанавливающую зрение при наследственной слепоте, получили премию Breakthrough Prize в области наук о жизни.  Их разработка позволила пациенту впервые увидеть лицо своего ребенка. Премия в размере 3 миллионов долларов признает их вклад в науку и медицину.
d23a3ac3d694.original_description:
Married couple Jean Bennett and Albert Maguire developed Luxturna, which helped a patient see their child’s face for the first time

A married couple who met over a dissected brain and went on to create the first approved gene therapy for blindness have been awarded one of the most lucrative prizes in science.

Molecular biologist Jean Bennett and ophthalmologist Albert Maguire share the $3m (£2.2m) Breakthrough prize for life sciences with physician Katherine High for the 25-year-l

632f02c06ed4.title: Simple “gut reset” may stop weight gain after Ozempic or Wegovy
632f02c06ed4.summary: A new minimally invasive procedure may help people keep weight off after stopping popular drugs like Ozempic and semaglutide—something most patients struggle with. In a clinical trial, those who underwent a technique called duodenal mucosal resurfacing regained far less weight compared to others after discontinuing the medication. The procedure works by renewing the lining of the upper small intestine, potentially “resetting” metabolism and preserving the benefits of weight loss.

99a77b971e85.title: This simple fatty acid could restore failing vision
99a77b971e85.summary: Scientists at UC Irvine have found a way to potentially reverse age-related vision loss by targeting the ELOVL2 “aging gene” and restoring vital fatty acids in the retina. Their experiments in mice show that supplementing with specific polyunsaturated fatty acids—not just DHA—can restore visual function and even reverse cellular aging signs.

038ffb6d7b9b.title: This new brain-like chip could slash AI energy use by 70%
038ffb6d7b9b.summary: Ученые разработали новый нейроморфный чип на основе оксида гафния, который имитирует работу нейронов и может значительно повысить энергоэффективность ИИ.  Новое устройство потребляет значительно меньше энергии, чем традиционные чипы, потенциально снижая энергопотребление ИИ до 70%. Это открытие может привести к созданию более экономичных и экологичных систем искусственного интеллекта.
038ffb6d7b9b.original_description: A breakthrough in brain-inspired computing could make today’s energy-hungry AI systems far more efficient. Researchers have engineered a new nanoelectronic device using a modified form of hafnium oxide that mimics how neurons process and store information at the same time. Unlike conventional chips that waste energy moving data back and forth, this device operates with ultra-low power—potentially slashing energy use by up to 70%.

2b50472f2e48.title: Scientists discover hidden forces are warping Earth deep beneath the surface
2b50472f2e48.summary: Scientists have mapped how Earth’s deepest mantle is being deformed—and the results point to long-lost tectonic plates buried thousands of kilometers underground. Using a massive global dataset of seismic waves, they found that most deformation happens in regions where these ancient slabs are thought to reside. The findings confirm long-standing theories but, for the first time, show the pattern on a global scale. It’s a major step toward understanding how the planet’s interior slowly churns ove

98fdddf05d05.title: Hidden virus in your gut may be linked to colon cancer
98fdddf05d05.summary: A newly discovered virus hiding inside a common gut bacterium could help explain one of medicine’s long-standing mysteries: why a microbe found in both healthy people and cancer patients is linked to colorectal cancer. The research suggests that the interaction between bacteria and the viruses they carry may be key to understanding disease risk. It may even lead to future screening tests that detect cancer risk earlier.

9b763b280bcb.title: This ancient crocodile relative grew up on four legs then walked on two
9b763b280bcb.summary: A bizarre crocodile relative from the age of dinosaurs is rewriting what scientists thought they knew about ancient reptiles. This poodle-sized creature, called Sonselasuchus cedrus, appears to have started life walking on all fours before shifting to a two-legged stance as it matured—an unusual transformation rarely seen in the fossil record.

ed51f8c35866.title: 289-million-year-old mummified reptile reveals how breathing began on land
ed51f8c35866.summary: A remarkably preserved, mummified reptile from 289 million years ago is rewriting what we know about how animals first breathed on land. This tiny creature, Captorhinus aguti, reveals the earliest known version of the rib-powered breathing system used by modern reptiles, birds, and mammals — a crucial innovation that helped vertebrates thrive outside water.

35c0a0e3e538.title: Scientists create plastic that destroys viruses on contact
35c0a0e3e538.summary: A new virus-fighting plastic film could transform everyday surfaces into invisible defenders against disease. Instead of relying on chemicals, this flexible material is covered in microscopic pillars that physically stretch viruses until they burst, rendering them harmless. In lab tests, it destroyed or disabled about 94% of virus particles within an hour, showing impressive effectiveness.

7f81c0fd7687.title: A simple blood test could reveal Alzheimer’s risk years early
7f81c0fd7687.summary: A routine blood marker tied to inflammation may reveal Alzheimer’s risk years in advance. Scientists found that higher neutrophil levels—part of the body’s first immune response—were linked to a greater chance of developing dementia. The discovery suggests this common lab value could help flag at-risk individuals before symptoms appear. It also raises the possibility that immune cells themselves may be fueling the disease.

f98222393ab7.title: Scientists stunned as JWST finds ice clouds on a giant alien planet
f98222393ab7.summary: Scientists have discovered unexpected water-ice clouds on a distant, Jupiter-like exoplanet, challenging current atmospheric models. By directly imaging Epsilon Indi Ab with the James Webb Space Telescope, they found less ammonia than expected—likely hidden by thick, patchy clouds. The finding reveals new layers of complexity in giant planets and shows how much we still have to learn.

e76de7c094c2.title: DNA reveals a hidden pitviper species in China
e76de7c094c2.summary: A vivid green pitviper hiding in Sichuan’s misty mountains has been revealed as a completely new species. Scientists had overlooked it for decades, assuming it was a common snake—until DNA analysis proved otherwise. Named after Laozi, it features striking differences between males and females, including bold stripes and eye colors. The discovery highlights just how many unknown species may still be lurking in well-studied regions.

1912075c61c3.title: Two common drugs may reverse fatty liver disease, study finds
1912075c61c3.summary: Scientists have discovered that combining two existing drugs can dramatically reduce liver fat linked to a common and often silent disease. The treatment not only improved liver health in animal models but also showed potential to lower heart-related risks. Interestingly, using lower doses of both drugs together worked just as well as higher doses alone. While promising, the findings still need to be tested in humans.

8e2f3e630000.title: “Baffling” new snake species in Myanmar looks like multiple species at once
8e2f3e630000.summary: Scientists have uncovered a fascinating new species of pit viper in Myanmar that seems to blur the very definition of what a species is. This snake, now named the Ayeyarwady pit viper, puzzled researchers because it looks like a mix between two known species—sometimes resembling one, sometimes the other, and occasionally something in between. Initially suspected to be a hybrid, genetic analysis revealed it is actually its own distinct species.

4d194fd17268.title: This “quantum” material fooled scientists and revealed something new
4d194fd17268.summary: A mysterious magnetic material once thought to host an exotic “quantum spin liquid” has turned out to be something entirely different—and possibly just as intriguing. Scientists studying cerium magnesium hexalluminate found it showed the hallmark signs of this elusive quantum state, like a lack of magnetic order and a spread of energy states. But after closer inspection using neutron experiments, they discovered the behavior came from a delicate tug-of-war between two opposing magnetic forces.

b734ddc518b2.title: This donut-shaped discovery just shattered a 150-year math rule
b734ddc518b2.summary: Ученые опровергли 150-летнее геометрическое правило, доказав существование двух разных математических объектов, имеющих форму пончика. Эти объекты идентичны при локальном измерении, но отличаются в целом, что меняет понимание связи между локальными и глобальными свойствами фигур.
b734ddc518b2.original_description: A 150-year-old rule in geometry has been proven wrong. Mathematicians found two different doughnut-shaped surfaces that look identical when measured locally but are actually different overall. For decades, researchers suspected this might be possible but couldn’t prove it—until now. The breakthrough reshapes how mathematicians understand the relationship between local measurements and global form.

dbeed5937589.title: A bizarre new state of matter may be hiding inside Uranus and Neptune
dbeed5937589.summary: Deep inside planets like Uranus and Neptune, scientists may have uncovered a bizarre new state of matter where atoms behave in unexpected ways. Advanced simulations suggest that carbon and hydrogen, under crushing pressures and scorching temperatures, can form a strange hybrid phase—part solid, part fluid—where hydrogen atoms spiral through a rigid carbon framework. This unusual “superionic” structure could reshape how heat and electricity flow inside these distant worlds, potentially helping ex

c389862994a1.title: This new camera captures what happens in a trillionth of a second
c389862994a1.summary: Ученые разработали новый метод визуализации, позволяющий запечатлевать события, происходящие за триллионные доли секунды. Технология не только измеряет яркость, но и фиксирует мельчайшие структурные изменения, превращая ультрабыстрые процессы в детальные "фильмы". Это открывает новые возможности для изучения плазмы, движения электронов и трансформации материалов в реальном времени.
c389862994a1.original_description: Scientists have unveiled a breakthrough imaging method that can capture the hidden details of events unfolding in trillionths of a second. This new technique doesn’t just track how bright something is—it also reveals subtle structural changes that were previously invisible, all in a single shot. By effectively turning ultrafast phenomena into detailed “movies,” researchers can now watch plasma form, electrons move, and materials transform in real time.

48754afdf036.title: Scientists just captured trees glowing with electricity during storms
48754afdf036.summary: Ученые впервые зафиксировали естественное свечение деревьев электричеством во время грозы.  Обнаруженные "коронные разряды" – слабые ультрафиолетовые вспышки на кончиках листьев – могут указывать на роль лесов в очистке воздуха, разрушая загрязняющие вещества.  Это открытие может существенно изменить понимание функционирования лесных экосистем.
48754afdf036.original_description: Scientists chasing thunderstorms in a retrofitted minivan finally captured something never seen before in nature: faint electrical glows shimmering from treetops during a storm. These “corona discharges,” long suspected but never observed outside a lab, appeared as tiny UV flashes at the tips of leaves. The discovery could reshape how we understand forests, since these bursts may help clean the air by breaking down pollutants.

e737d24e84b8.title: AI just revealed ocean currents we’ve never been able to see
e737d24e84b8.summary: Новый метод, основанный на искусственном интеллекте (AI) под названием GOFLOW, позволяет создавать детальные карты океанических течений из изображений с метеорологических спутников.  Он обнаруживает ранее невидимые, быстротечные течения, анализируя изменения температуры, что важно для понимания климата, экосистем и хранения углерода. Технология использует существующие спутники, делая ее эффективной и экономичной.
e737d24e84b8.original_description: A new AI-driven method called GOFLOW is turning weather satellite images into highly detailed maps of ocean currents. By tracking how temperature patterns shift over time, it can reveal fast-moving, small-scale currents that were previously impossible to observe directly. These currents are key to understanding climate, marine ecosystems, and carbon storage. The breakthrough works using satellites already in orbit, making it both powerful and cost-effective.

cd4a516aaaca.title: Ancient DNA reveals a lost population near Paris replaced by strangers
cd4a516aaaca.summary: Ancient DNA from a tomb near Paris reveals a shocking prehistoric reset: one population vanished and was replaced by newcomers from the south. The two groups show no genetic connection, signaling a major upheaval around 3000 BC. Disease, including early plague, likely played a role, but wasn’t the only cause. The change also reshaped society, ending tightly knit family burials and coinciding with the disappearance of Europe’s megalith builders.

Size does matter – as does diet – but your genes are the main driver of your cholesterol levels

Cholesterol, a fatty substance mostly made by the liver and used by the body to build cells and produce hormones, has become a heart-health bogeyman. 9378b5255de2.title: George Ariyoshi, first US governor of Asian American descent, dies aged 100
9378b5255de2.summary:
Democrat led Hawaii from 1973 to 1986, coinciding with the party’s rise to power in the state

George R Ariyoshi – Hawaii’s former governor and the nation’s first Asian American governor – has died at age 100.

Ariyoshi, a Democrat who led the state from 1973 to 1986, died peacefully while surrounded by family on Sunday night, according to a statement Monday from the current governor, Josh Green.

Andy Warhol sent Paul a Brillo box. Fran Lebowitz called Peter “a genius about sex”. The ending of Susan Sontag’s second novel was inspired by a bunch of Peter’s photographs.
